World Commander — command arena report

Natural-language command of agents in real time, under a latency budget. Phase-1 warm-up of the World Commander program.

Below is the arena in motion: coloured agents follow streamed natural-language orders while grey agents wander on their own clock. Press Play (or drag the slider) to watch what happens — then read on for what is measured.

Grid replay (you control the speed — drag the slider or press Play)

command 0 / 0

How to read the grid

  • Coloured agents — the ones you command (red, blue, green, yellow). The letter is the colour’s initial.
  • Grey agents — uncontrolled NPCs (marked “·”). They move one random step every tick on their own clock; you cannot command them. They are what makes a late command cost something.
  • Gold ring — the agent(s) the current command targets.
  • Fanned-out markers in one cell — several agents on the same square (the world has no collision rule), spread apart so none is hidden.
  • Axes — x is the column (0–7, left→right), y is the row (top→bottom); “north” decreases y. The title shows the command, whether it was grounded, and its latency.

What this is

The command arena is a minimal grid world that stress-tests one thing: can a language model turn streamed natural-language orders into the right moves fast enough to matter. Each step, one order is issued (e.g. “Move the red agent north”); the model reads the world state and replies with the moves. One order is trivial by design — the test is the stream, many and fast. Crucially the clock never pauses: the grey agents move on their own every step, so a late command concedes ground, exactly as a slow decision concedes to an opponent in a real game.

Results at a glance

90 commands · grounding 1.00 · deadline miss 0.70 · latency p50 919 / p95 1285 ms

Grounding is whether the moves were correct; deadline miss is whether they arrived within the tick budget. The model is accurate but often late — the definitions and the per-command-type breakdown below show where the time goes.

Run configuration

ModelQwen/Qwen3-14B-AWQ
Grid8 × 8
Controlled agents4 (colour-tagged: red, blue, green, yellow)
Uncontrolled agents (NPCs)4 (grey; move one random step each tick)
Tick budget500 ms — the per-command deadline
Commands in this run90
Seed0 (world + command stream are reproducible)

Metrics — what each number means

Grounding accuracy
Fraction of commands where the model’s move set exactly matches the ground-truth move set (right agents, right direction). Measures understanding, independent of speed.
Command-to-action latency
Wall-clock time (ms) from issuing the command to the model returning its action. Reported as mean, median (p50), and 95th percentile (p95).
Deadline miss rate
Fraction of commands whose latency exceeded the 500 ms tick budget. A late action is dropped — it simply does not happen — and the world ticks on regardless. This is the real-time penalty: correct but late still loses.

Latency by command type

The arena issues three command forms: single-target, a positively named multi-agent subset (“the blue and green agents”), and the all-except group. Multi-agent orders name more agents, so the model emits more tokens and takes longer — the usual source of a high-latency cluster.

Command typecountmean latencydeadline miss
single-target
“Move the red agent north.”		28434 ms0.04
positive subset
“Move the blue and green agents north.”		281013 ms1.00
all-except group
“Every agent except the red one, move north.”		341006 ms1.00

The charts

Top — latency distribution. Histogram of per-command latency. The dashed red line is the 500 ms tick budget; everything to its right is a deadline miss. Dotted lines mark mean / p50 / p95. A split (bimodal) shape means two populations of commands — typically fast single-target vs slower multi-agent orders.
Bottom — latency over the stream. One dot per command in issue order; green = on time, red = deadline miss. Shows whether misses are scattered or clustered as the stream runs.

Deadline frontier

A deadline miss is simply latency > deadline, so this whole curve is computed post-hoc from one run — no extra inference. It shows the latency budget the model needs instead of fixing a single, arbitrary deadline. At the current 500 ms, single-target commands are feasible while multi-agent ones are not; multi-agent commands need roughly 1.5 s to arrive on time. This performance-vs-budget frontier is the benchmark’s real output — any single deadline is just one vertical slice of it, and it shifts with model size, GPU, and output verbosity.

Output-schema comparison

schemagroundingp50 latencymiss@500msmulti p50multi miss@500ms
json1.00920 ms0.70949 ms1.00
pairs1.00298 ms0.03320 ms0.05
grouped1.00259 ms0.01278 ms0.02

The same task run under different reply formats. A terser schema emits fewer output tokens, so its frontier sits to the left (the model meets tighter deadlines). The effect is largest on multi-agent commands, whose latency is dominated by output length. Watch grounding too: a format the model follows less reliably trades accuracy for speed.

World Commander — Phase-1 command arena. Model: Qwen/Qwen3-14B-AWQ. Self-contained report (images and frames embedded); regenerate with scripts/visualize.py. Grounding/latency are real measurements; a late action is dropped under the unpausable clock.
